svg.text: 1. Orbital Shapes — Why Each Subshell Has a Fixed Capacity
Each orbital = 1 region of space holding exactly 2 electrons (opposite spins). Capacity = 2 × number of orientations.
Each orbital holds exactly 2 electrons with opposite spins (Pauli exclusion principle).
Electrons fill the lowest available energy subshell first. The diagonal grid (left) shows which subshells exist. The numbered list (right) gives the correct filling order.
Key insight: 4s fills BEFORE 3d (#6 vs #7 in the list) — because 4s has lower energy for Z < 21.
This is why K (Z=19) is [Ar]4s¹ and not [Ar]3d¹. The energy ordering is not simply "shell number".
3. Energy Level Crossing — Why 4s Fills Before 3d (but Empties First)

As atomic number Z increases, subshell energies shift. 4s dips below 3d for light atoms, then 3d drops back below 4s for heavier atoms. This resembles — but is NOT — hysteresis: it is a smooth, one-way energy inversion as Z increases.
Hysteresis would require the system to return via a different path. Here the crossing is permanent and monotonic — it is an energy level inversion, not a cycle.
Fe is [Ar]3d⁶4s² — but when ionised to Fe²⁺, it loses the 4s electrons FIRST, giving [Ar]3d⁶ (not [Ar]3d⁴4s²).
Once 3d starts filling, it drops below 4s in energy. The 4s electrons become the highest-energy ones — they leave first.
4. Hund's Rule — How Electrons Fill a Subshell
Within a subshell, each orbital is occupied singly before any pairing (electrons prefer empty seats). Two electrons sharing an orbital must have opposite spins ↑↓ (Pauli exclusion).
Must now pair — 4th e⁻ pairs up
Hund's Rule — electrons are like bus passengers: they take an empty seat before sitting next to someone.
This minimises repulsion — same-spin electrons in separate orbitals stay physically further apart.
Half-filled (3d⁵) and fully-filled (3d¹⁰) subshells have extra stability — one electron "steals" from 4s.
